title: Strategy and Investment in Rural Housing Preservation Act of 2025
short_title: Strategy and Investment in Rural Housing Preservation Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill creates a program to help save affordable apartment buildings in rural areas that were built with government money. The program helps building owners change their loans and keep rent low for families who don't have much money, including farm workers. If some buildings can't stay affordable anymore, the bill gives housing vouchers to help families pay for other places to live. The bill also requires the Secretary of Agriculture to make a plan and set up a group of advisors to figure out the best ways to keep rural housing affordable for people who need it.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Strategy and Investment in Rural Housing Preservation Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Permanent Establishment of Housing Preservation and Revitalization Program
sections.text: This section creates a new program to save and improve apartment buildings in rural areas. The Secretary of Agriculture will run this program. The program helps apartment buildings that were built with government loans under sections five hundred fourteen, five hundred fifteen, or five hundred sixteen of existing housing law. When these loans are close to ending, the Secretary must tell both property owners and renters what will happen. Property owners get written notice four years before their loan ends, explaining their choices and help available. Renters get written notice three years before the loan ends, telling them when the loan ends, what might happen to their home, and how to get help finding another place to live or get a housing voucher. The notice must be written in plain English and translated to other languages if many residents speak those languages. The Secretary can change loan terms to help keep these apartments affordable. This includes lowering or removing interest, delaying payments, reducing debt, and giving other money help. When loans are changed, the Secretary will offer to renew rental assistance contracts for up to twenty years, as long as Congress provides money each year. The property owner must agree to keep the building safe and in good condition. If some renters do not have rental assistance, the Secretary can give them help too. Property owners must sign agreements to keep operating these apartments according to housing rules. These agreements last as long as the loan or twenty years, whichever is longer. If a loan cannot be changed but the building has rental assistance, the Secretary can still renew the rental assistance contract for twenty years. Rent will be based on either what the building needs to operate or a standard payment amount, whichever is less. Property owners must submit a plan showing how they will pay for repairs and improvements. If the Secretary does not respond within thirty days, the rental assistance is automatically approved for one year. The Secretary can give grants to nonprofit groups and public housing agencies to help property owners keep these apartments affordable. The Secretary will create a process to help renters move their rental assistance to another property or get a housing voucher if their current building stops being affordable. The Secretary can spend up to one million dollars each year on running this program. Congress is asked to provide two hundred million dollars each year from two thousand twenty-six through two thousand thirty for this program. The Secretary must publish proposed rules within one hundred eighty days and final temporary rules within one year of this law taking effect.

sections.title: Section 3 Eligibility for Rural Housing Vouchers
sections.text: This section allows the Secretary to give rural housing vouchers to low-income families living in certain apartment buildings. These vouchers help pay rent. Families can get vouchers if they live in buildings that got government loans or grants under sections five hundred fourteen, five hundred fifteen, or five hundred sixteen, and the building has been paid off early without restrictions, been foreclosed on, or had its loan end after September thirtieth two thousand five without getting rental assistance under the new program.

sections.title: Section 4 Amount of Voucher Assistance
sections.text: This section says that rural housing vouchers will follow the payment rules already set in section five hundred forty-two of existing housing law, regardless of what other laws might say about payment amounts.

sections.title: Section 5 Rental Assistance Contract Authority
sections.text: This section changes existing law about rental assistance contracts. When property owners of buildings with government loans under sections five hundred fourteen or five hundred fifteen ask for help, or when owners have signed agreements under the new preservation program, the Secretary can renew their rental assistance contracts for twenty years or the length of their loan, whichever is shorter. Congress must provide money each year for these contracts. When a renter moves out and loses rental assistance, the property owner gets at least one year to give that assistance to another eligible renter in the same building.

sections.title: Section 6 Funding for Multifamily Technical Improvements
sections.text: Congress is asked to provide fifty million dollars in two thousand twenty-six for the Department of Agriculture to improve its computer systems used to process and manage loans for apartment buildings. These improvements must be completed within five years, and the money stays available for that entire five-year period.

sections.title: Section 7 Plan for Preserving Affordability of Rental Projects
sections.text: Within six months of this law taking effect, the Secretary of Agriculture must give Congress a written plan for keeping apartment buildings affordable for low-income families and preventing renters from being forced to move. The plan must include specific goals, ways to reach those goals, ways to measure progress, detailed reporting on results, and recommendations for new laws if needed. The Secretary must create an advisory committee of sixteen people to help with this plan and the preservation program. The committee includes government officials, property developers and owners, state housing agencies, renters, community development financial institutions, nonprofit organizations, tax credit investors, banks, and farmworker advocacy groups. The committee must meet at least once every three months. The committee will help estimate the size and condition of rural rental housing, review and improve policies for saving affordable housing, monitor program results, and report to Congress and the public. The Department of Agriculture can use its administrative money to pay for committee members to travel to meetings.
